role_summary: Chicano civil rights organizer associated with the San Diego, California civil rights network; in this corpus's embedding his testimony pairs most strongly with Harold K. Brown (San Diego CORE) and clusters with both Black and Chicano movement voices.
ai_reading: Camarillo's top semantic neighbor is Harold K. Brown (entry 55, count 511), the San Diego CORE founder and Black civil rights leader; this 511 count is among the catalog's strongest cross-ethnic cosine pairings. The pairing confirms the cross-racial cohort the Brown page itself identifies and gives it a bidirectional signature (Brown's own top neighbors include Camarillo at 178). Camarillo's remaining neighbors include Gloria Arellanes (entry 47, Brown Berets), Maria Varela (entry 81, SNCC + Ganados del Valle), Roberta Alexander (entry 110, Berkeley BPP), and Robert L. Carter (entry 108, NAACP LDF). The embedding therefore reads him as a cross-racial movement bridge voice. The catalog's most-striking observation about him is his Sacred Framing vs Secular Framing position: -1.00, the MAXIMUM toward Sacred Framing in the entire 136-interview corpus. For a Chicano civil rights organizer the most-Sacred placement is unexpected within the catalog's broader pattern (most movement-religious voices are SCLC-adjacent or Black-Baptist), possibly catching Liberation Theology vocabulary or the Catholic-rooted strands of Chicano movement organizing the cultural reception filters out. His +0.71 Tactical Pragmatism vs Strategic Vision position is also worth noting: he reads as strongly strategic-vision-oriented, alongside a 511-cosine pairing with a Black civil rights organizer his cultural placement would not have predicted as a close intellectual kin.
biographical_paragraph: Mateo Camarillo's oral history is held at the Library of Congress as entry 86 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1]. Independent biographical sourcing under Wikipedia returned 404 for the slug variant searched in this iteration; the strongest signal currently available for his movement role is the embedding's tight bidirectional cosine with Harold K. Brown (entry 55), the San Diego CORE founder whose own page documents the city's Black civil rights leadership of the 1960s, and the cross-racial bridging cluster of Gloria Arellanes, Maria Varela, and Roberta Alexander visible in his neighbors.
movement_context: The Mexican American Youth Association formations of the 1960s, MAYO in San Antonio led by Jose Angel Gutierrez, Mario Compean, and Willie Velasquez and parallel MAYO bodies in San Diego, Los Angeles, and the Bay Area, were the youth-organizing infrastructure that the broader Chicano Movement of 1965-1975 grew through; the San Diego cohort built its 1960s organizing on barrio-level civic-action work, anti-police-brutality response, and the bilingual-education and college-access advocacy that the South Texas MAYO had pioneered in 1967 [src: 2, src: 3]. The Chicano Federation of San Diego, the umbrella civic-services coordinating body that consolidated barrio-level service work in the late 1960s and remains in operation as a federally qualified provider today, was the institutional home for much of San Diego's Mexican American organizing of the period and the connecting node between MAYO youth activism, the Catholic-parish networks of Logan Heights and Barrio Logan, and the San Diego CORE cohort led by Harold K. Brown (catalog entry 55) [src: 2]. The broader Chicano Movement cohort the embedding places Camarillo within draws together Gloria Arellanes (catalog entry 47), Minister of Finance and Correspondence of the Los Angeles Brown Berets and the first female Prime Minister of the organization, who joined the National Chicano Moratorium Committee in 1969 and helped organize the August 29, 1970 march at Laguna Park; Rodolfo Corky Gonzales, founder of the Crusade for Justice in Denver in 1966 and author of the 1967 epic poem Yo Soy Joaquin that became a movement-defining literary text, whose Crusade hosted the March 1969 First National Chicano Youth Liberation Conference that produced El Plan Espiritual de Aztlan, the foundational ideological framework for the Chicano student movement [src: 3, src: 4]; Cesar Chavez and Dolores Huerta, co-founders of the National Farm Workers Association in 1962 (renamed the United Farm Workers in 1972), whose 1965-1970 Delano grape strike and nationwide consumer grape boycott was the movement's most sustained labor-organizing campaign and produced the first table-grape collective-bargaining agreements in California agricultural history [src: 2]; and Reies Lopez Tijerina, founder of the Alianza Federal de Mercedes in 1963 in New Mexico, whose June 5, 1967 Tierra Amarilla courthouse raid in pursuit of Spanish and Mexican land-grant restoration radicalized the movement's land-rights wing and made Tijerina one of its most-prosecuted figures [src: 2, src: 3]. The August 29, 1970 National Chicano Moratorium march against the Vietnam War, organized by the National Chicano Moratorium Committee that Arellanes joined in 1969 and that drew the movement's geographic-cohort organizers from Los Angeles, San Diego, Denver, San Antonio, and the Bay Area to a single rally at Laguna Park, drew 20,000 to 30,000 participants and was broken up in the afternoon by Los Angeles County Sheriff's deputies; Los Angeles Times columnist Ruben Salazar was killed at the Silver Dollar Cafe by a sheriff's-deputy tear-gas projectile fired through the bar's doorway, an event the movement understood as the deliberate elimination of its most prominent journalistic voice [src: 5, src: 4]. La Raza Unida Party, founded in 1970 by Jose Angel Gutierrez and Mario Compean in Crystal City, Texas as the Chicano third-party political vehicle and which by 1972 ran a Texas gubernatorial candidate and operated chapters across California, Colorado, and New Mexico, was the period's most-developed electoral expression of the same youth-organizing cohort that the regional MAYO bodies had produced [src: 3].
legacy_and_reception: The Chicano Federation of San Diego, the institutional vehicle the late-1960s San Diego MAYO and parish-network organizing built, has operated continuously since its 1969 founding and today runs early-childhood-education, family-resource, housing-assistance, and behavioral-health programs across San Diego County under federal qualified-community-services designations; the institutional continuity from the period of Camarillo's movement work to the contemporary social-services infrastructure of the city's Mexican American community is one of the more durable organizational records of the broader Chicano Movement, paralleling the descent of the East L.A. Free Clinic into AltaMed Health Services that Arellanes's catalog page documents [src: 2, src: 6]. San Diego's Chicano Park in Barrio Logan, established by community land-occupation on April 22, 1970 after the city moved to construct a California Highway Patrol substation on land that had been promised as a community park beneath the Coronado Bridge, became the period's most-visible architectural marker of San Diego Chicano organizing; the park's outdoor murals, painted across the bridge pylons from 1973 onward by Salvador Roberto Torres, Mario Torero, Victor Ochoa, Michael Schnorr, and a rotating community of muralists, were designated a National Historic Landmark by the National Park Service in January 2017 and remain the largest collection of outdoor Chicano-movement murals in the United States [src: 6, src: 7]. Camarillo's 2013 Library of Congress oral history at catalog entry 86 sits within the broader 136-interview Civil Rights History Project corpus produced by the American Folklife Center under accession AFC 2010/039 and represents the project's San Diego cohort of voices alongside Harold K. Brown (catalog entry 55), with whom his testimony pairs at the catalog's strongest cross-ethnic cosine (511); the bidirectional pairing is itself a piece of archival evidence for the cross-racial San Diego organizing the two careers documented in their lifetimes [src: 1].

gallery.alt: 1969 National Chicano Moratorium Committee pinback button against the Vietnam War
gallery.caption: Pinback button produced in 1969 by the National Chicano Moratorium Committee, the organizing body whose August 29, 1970 march at Laguna Park drew 20,000 to 30,000 marchers from across the Southwest including the San Diego organizing cohort the embedding places Camarillo within.

gallery.alt: Jose Angel Gutierrez and Mario Compean, La Raza Unida Party, 1970
gallery.caption: Jose Angel Gutierrez and Mario Compean photographed in 1970 around the founding of La Raza Unida Party in Crystal City, Texas. Gutierrez and Compean had previously co-founded the South Texas Mexican American Youth Organization (MAYO) in 1967, the same MAYO youth-organizing formation that gave rise to parallel cohorts in San Diego, Los Angeles, and the Bay Area, including the San Diego network within which Camarillo organized in the late 1960s.
gallery.license: Public domain (U.S. publication 1931-1977 without copyright notice)

gallery.alt: Undocumented Worker mural by Michael Schnorr at Chicano Park, Barrio Logan, San Diego
gallery.caption: The Undocumented Worker mural by Michael Schnorr at Chicano Park in Barrio Logan, San Diego, originally painted in 1980 and restored in 2011. Chicano Park was established by community land-occupation on April 22, 1970 after the city moved to construct a California Highway Patrol substation on land promised as a community park beneath the Coronado Bridge; the park's outdoor murals on the bridge pylons remain the largest collection of Chicano-movement murals in the United States and were designated a National Historic Landmark in January 2017.

sources.title: Mateo Camarillo oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: Ernesto Chavez, Mi Raza Primero! (My People First!): Nationalism, Identity, and Insurgency in the Chicano Movement in Los Angeles, 1966-1978
sources.publisher: University of California Press, 2002; WorldCat OCLC 48810209 (institutional history of the East Los Angeles Chicano movement and its organizational architecture; documents the MAYO youth-organizing formations of the late 1960s, the Brown Berets, the National Chicano Moratorium Committee, and the cross-regional structure within which the San Diego cohort organized)

sources.title: Carlos Munoz Jr., Youth, Identity, Power: The Chicano Movement
sources.publisher: Verso Books, 1989; revised and expanded edition Verso 2007; WorldCat OCLC 22305158 (the foundational synthetic history of the Chicano student generation; documents the MAYO origins, the 1969 El Plan de Santa Barbara conference that produced the MEChA national organizing framework, and the broader youth-political infrastructure of the period)

sources.title: Maylei Blackwell, Chicana Power!: Contested Histories of Feminism in the Chicano Movement
sources.publisher: University of Texas Press, 2011; WorldCat OCLC 693810471 (the archival history of Chicana feminist organizing within and beyond the Brown Berets; documents the women-led organizational precedents that connected the regional Chicano-movement bodies of the period)

sources.title: Ernesto B. Vigil, The Crusade for Justice: Chicano Militancy and the Government's War on Dissent
sources.publisher: University of Wisconsin Press, 1999; WorldCat OCLC 40500587 (insider archival history of Rodolfo Corky Gonzales's Denver-based Crusade for Justice from its 1966 founding through the 1970s; documents the March 1969 First National Chicano Youth Liberation Conference and the federal surveillance and counterintelligence operations against the broader movement infrastructure)

sources.title: Lorena Oropeza, Raza Si! Guerra No!: Chicano Protest and Patriotism during the Viet Nam War Era
sources.publisher: University of California Press, 2005; WorldCat OCLC 52557571 (institutional history of the Chicano antiwar movement and the National Chicano Moratorium Committee; documents the August 29, 1970 march at Laguna Park, the Los Angeles County Sheriff's Department's breakup of the rally, and the Ruben Salazar killing at the Silver Dollar Cafe)

sources.title: Mario T. Garcia, Mexican Americans: Leadership, Ideology, and Identity, 1930-1960
sources.publisher: Yale University Press, 1989; WorldCat OCLC 45576939 (the institutional and intellectual prehistory of the 1960s Chicano Movement; documents the Mexican American civic-organizing and middle-class leadership networks of the preceding generation against which the late-1960s MAYO and Brown Berets cohorts defined their generational politics)

sources.title: Mario T. Garcia, The Chicano Generation: Testimonios of the Movement
sources.publisher: University of California Press, 2015; WorldCat OCLC 889325378 (testimonio-based archive of the East L.A. and Southern California Chicano-movement cohort; the chapter on Chicana women in the Brown Berets anchored on Gloria Arellanes's testimonio documents the cross-regional cohort within which the embedding places Camarillo)

sources.title: Civil Rights History Project collection finding aid, Mateo Camarillo entry
sources.publisher: American Folklife Center, Library of Congress (collection-level metadata for AFC 2010/039 including the field-recording date, the interview transcript, and the related collection materials)
